questions.question: You benchmark a model and report 'average 12 ms per image on a 4090'. What is missing from this measurement?
options: Nothing; average latency on the target GPU is a complete and actionable benchmark

Percentiles (p50/p95/p99), warmup discipline, and CUDA synchronisation: a mean hides tail latency that causes real-time failures, and without `torch.cuda.synchronize()` you measure kernel dispatch rather than kernel execution
The model size in MB, which is required for a complete latency report
The dataset name used as the input source during profiling
questions.explanation: Production latency is measured in percentiles because real-time systems fail on tails, not averages. Without warmup, JIT compilation inflates the first few measurements. Without `torch.cuda.synchronize()`, GPU kernel launches return before the kernel finishes, so you measure dispatch, not execution. All three have to be right for the number to be meaningful.

questions.question: In `measure_latency`, 10 dummy forward passes run before the timed loop begins. What specific problem does this warmup solve?
options: It allocates GPU VRAM ahead of time so no page faults occur during the timed region
It verifies the model produces numerically correct outputs before profiling begins

It eliminates JIT compilation overhead and cold-cache effects from the measured timings — the first few calls trigger kernel compilation and cache loading that would otherwise inflate every benchmark number
It calibrates the `time.perf_counter()` clock against the GPU hardware counter
questions.explanation: PyTorch compiles CUDA kernels lazily. The first one or two forward passes for a given model and input shape trigger kernel compilation and data-cache warming, producing unrepresentative runtimes. Warmup ensures the timed iterations measure steady-state inference — the number that actually predicts production behaviour. The `measure_latency` function also synchronises the GPU around each timed call for this reason.

questions.question: Post-training static INT8 quantisation requires a calibration dataset, but dynamic quantisation does not. Why?
options: Dynamic quantisation only quantises weights to INT8 at load time and computes activations in floating-point, so it never needs to observe actual activation distributions — the weight ranges are determined analytically from the stored FP32 values
Static quantisation uses a large built-in calibration dataset from PyTorch, while dynamic quantisation uses a user-provided one
Dynamic quantisation actually does require calibration data, but PyTorch hides this step inside `torch.ao.quantization.prepare`
Dynamic quantisation is only applicable to transformer models and skips the activation quantisation step entirely
questions.explanation: Dynamic quantisation converts weights to INT8 once at load time but computes every activation in FP32 (or FP16). Because activations are never quantised, there is nothing to calibrate. Static quantisation quantises both weights and activations, so it must observe real input data to record the min/max activation range for every layer — that is what the calibration loop does. This is why static PTQ delivers bigger speedups but requires a calibration set.

questions.question: The `quantise_ptq` function calls `tq.prepare(model, inplace=True)` before running the calibration loop through `calibration_loader`. What does `prepare` do to the model?
options: It immediately converts all FP32 weight tensors to INT8 using per-layer symmetric quantisation
It fuses Conv-BN-ReLU triplets into single quantisation-friendly modules before any observers are attached
It freezes all model parameters to prevent accidental gradient updates during the calibration pass

It inserts observer modules (min-max or histogram) at every quantisable layer's input and output; during the calibration forward passes these observers record activation statistics that `tq.convert` later uses to select INT8 scale and zero-point values
questions.explanation: `tq.prepare` wraps each quantisable layer with observer hooks. During the calibration loop, the observers record min and max (or a full histogram) of the activation values seen on real data. `tq.convert` then reads those statistics to set the INT8 quantisation parameters (scale, zero-point) for each layer. Skipping calibration and guessing these values is the most common cause of large accuracy drops after quantisation.

questions.question: Post-training static INT8 quantisation typically results in what accuracy loss on ImageNet-class vision models when properly applied?
options: 5–10 percentage points, requiring QAT to recover
20+ percentage points; INT8 is generally unusable for vision without QAT
Exactly 0; post-training static quantisation is always lossless on vision models

0.1–1 percentage point when properly calibrated — BatchNorm-fused conv models are particularly well-behaved, and the main failure modes are insufficient calibration data or un-fused BatchNorm layers producing large activation outliers
questions.explanation: Static PTQ on well-calibrated vision models loses a fraction of a point to about 1 point. The main failure modes are insufficient calibration data, quantising activations with extreme outliers (fix with per-channel quantisation or clipping), and un-fused BatchNorm. When those are handled, INT8 delivers ~4× model-size reduction and 2–4× compute speedup for essentially free accuracy cost.

questions.question: You export a PyTorch model to ONNX with opset 17 and it fails with 'Unsupported operator'. What are the most likely root causes?
options: The installed ONNX library version is too old and needs upgrading to support opset 17

(1) A custom op or call to `torch.special.*` with no ONNX mapping; (2) a Python-level control-flow branch that tracing cannot capture; (3) a tensor-scalar interaction that is implicit in PyTorch but has no ONNX node. Fixes: replace the unsupported op, use `torch.jit.script` for control flow, or upgrade opset
The model has too many parameters for the ONNX protobuf format to represent
The input shape uses a dynamic dimension that ONNX does not support
questions.explanation: ONNX export is tracing by default, so anything tracing cannot capture — runtime control flow, Python branches, custom CUDA ops, calls to torch.special functions added after the opset was frozen — fails. Most real failures are a single node with no ONNX equivalent. The fix is either replacing the op, switching to `torch.jit.script` for control-flow-heavy code, or moving to a newer opset where the op exists. Dynamic input shapes are handled via `dynamic_axes`, not an opset issue.
